message.content:
You are Fable 5, ONE of two independent designers (the other is Sol — do NOT coordinate). Design the Research Planning Gate for our champion deep-research pipeline.

FIRST read these two files in full:
- Brief: /tmp/claude-1000/-home-polaris-polaris-project/21e87760-8436-4090-870d-99ef2121882e/scratchpad/reviews/gate_brief.md
- 2026 best-practices digest: /tmp/claude-1000/-home-polaris-polaris-project/21e87760-8436-4090-870d-99ef2121882e/scratchpad/reviews/gate_bestpractices_digest.md

THEN explore the champion code to ground your design in real file:function hooks. The champion repo is /home/polaris/wt/outline_agent (branch bot/outline-agent-box = the 0.4447 champion). Key code: src/polaris_graph/retrieval/fs_researcher_query_gen.py (FS-Researcher query gen), src/polaris_graph/outline/outline_agent.py (run_outline_agent_or_legacy — the existing 'deep think' planner that searches ~100+ more URLs), scripts/compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py (driver), src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py (generate_multi_section_report ~line 3010 has deliverable_spec/scope_spec seams). provenance_generator.py holds the FROZEN verifier — READ ONLY, never touch it. The validated rule-reader we already built lives on branch round1-if-compiler: view with `cd /home/polaris/wt/outline_agent && git show round1-if-compiler:src/polaris_graph/instruction/constraint_extractor.py`. You may WebFetch a source from the digest if you want to verify a detail.

Follow the brief's Output format exactly (7 sections). Emphasize the four critical questions: (A) autonomous vs interactive split — the DeepResearch Bench has NO human to answer clarifications or click Continue, so design both modes; (B) your recommendation on the gate<->outline-agent seam (replace / feed / wrap — pick one and justify); (C) ask-vs-assume policy; (D) no-starvation — scope must reach FS-Researcher's query generation, never just filter a frozen corpus down (we proved filtering 997→131 rows tanks RACE 0.4447→0.3264). Faithfulness is FROZEN — the gate is entirely upstream and must never touch strict_verify.

Return your COMPLETE design as your final message (markdown, the 7 sections from the brief). This is data for an Opus consolidation, not a human-facing note — be thorough and concrete.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# DESIGN BRIEF — the Research Planning Gate (independent design review)
2	
3	You are one of two independent designers (the other is {OTHER}). Do NOT coordinate. Produce a rigorous, buildable design. Cite `file:function` when you reference our code. Say "I don't know / needs a spike" rather than hand-wave.
4	
5	## The goal
6	Design a **Research Planning Gate**: a front stage that runs BEFORE any expensive retrieval, deeply understands an arbitrary user prompt, and produces a structured, user-approvable research plan that then guardrails every downstream stage so the final report fits the prompt with **zero drift** — on ANY prompt, not just benchmark task 72.
7	
8	This is the pattern ChatGPT Deep Research / Gemini Deep Research / Claude use: a small reasoning step that clarifies intent, proposes a research plan (direction + scope + outline), lets the user edit or approve, then runs. We want our own version.
9	
10	```
11	User prompt
12	  → [GATE: understand deeply] decompose multiple folded-in questions; surface ambiguity;
13	      infer implicit intent (audience, purpose, depth); extract the full contract
14	  → [PROPOSE plan]: research direction + scope + outline + assumptions + clarifying questions
15	  → user EDITS (wrong) or APPROVES/Continue (right)
16	  → deep research runs; the approved plan is the guardrail for retrieval → outline → compose → render
17	```
18	
19	## What you must DESIGN (deliverables)
20	1. **The understanding engine.** How the gate reads a prompt to produce: (a) decomposed sub-questions (a compound prompt → N research threads); (b) the full **contract** — every dimension a prompt can impose: source scope (type, quality bar, language, recency window, geography, domain, prohibited sources), content requirements (required topics/coverage, must-address questions, entities include/exclude, required comparisons, breadth vs depth), presentation (document type, structure, length, required sections, tables/figures), tone & audience, meta (citation style, hedging, POV); (c) ambiguity flags + implicit-intent inferences, each marked EXPLICIT vs INFERRED; (d) assumptions. Is it one LLM call or a small reasoning loop? Give the actual prompt/schema shape.
21	2. **The plan artifact** the user reviews/edits — its data model, what is editable, and how edits flow back into the contract.
22	3. **Per-stage guardrail map** — how each approved contract term is ENFORCED at: FS-Researcher/retrieval (scope → queries + ingestion filter), the outline agent (required coverage + approved outline), compose (tone/style), render (format/length/tables), and a final **compliance audit** (did the output satisfy every term — this is NOT faithfulness).
23	
24	## The CRITICAL questions you MUST answer (these are where it breaks)
25	- **A. Benchmark has NO human.** DeepResearch Bench hands over a prompt and wants a report — nobody answers clarifications or clicks Continue. So the gate needs TWO modes: **(i) interactive** (real users: ask, show plan, edit, approve) and **(ii) autonomous** (benchmark / "run with best guess": infer the whole contract, make best assumptions, proceed WITHOUT asking). Design both. Without mode (ii) the gate cannot be RACE-scored. This is the single most important constraint.
26	- **B. Relationship to the EXISTING outline agent.** The champion already has an outline agent that "deeply thinks" and searches more evidence (`src/polaris_graph/outline/outline_agent.py`, `run_outline_agent_or_legacy`; the driver runs it via PG_OUTLINE_AGENT=1). Does the new gate REPLACE its planning, FEED it a locked contract + approved outline, or WRAP it? Design the exact seam so two planners don't fight. Recommend one.
27	- **C. When to ASK vs ASSUME.** Real products ask 1–3 questions max. Give the policy for "worth a clarifying question" vs "state an assumption the user can override." Operator rules: NEVER invent a constraint the user didn't state; prefer fresher sources when recency unspecified (soft, not a hard cutoff).
28	- **D. No starvation.** Scope must drive retrieval to GO FIND enough in-scope sources — never filter a fixed pool down. (We already proved filtering the frozen 997-row unscoped corpus down to 131 journal rows starves the report: RACE 0.4447 → 0.3264, and even Instruction-Following fell.) The scope contract must reach FS-Researcher's query generation.
29	- **E. Generality.** Validate the design against very different prompts: single vs multi-question, tightly-scoped vs wide-open, and across several of the 100 DRB tasks — not just task 72.
30	
31	## Hard rails (non-negotiable)
32	- **FAITHFULNESS IS FROZEN.** The gate is entirely upstream; it must NEVER touch `strict_verify` / the citation verifier / the drop rule (`src/polaris_graph/generator/provenance_generator.py`). No new verification, no new faithfulness test.
33	- Priority: **COVERAGE > INSIGHT > READABILITY**.
34	- **Reuse, don't rebuild.** A validated constraint extractor (the "rule-reader") already exists on branch `round1-if-compiler @ f2b0d5e` (`src/polaris_graph/instruction/constraint_extractor.py`) — it correctly extracted journal-only/English/4 coverage topics/literature_review/academic tone from task 72. Reuse/extend it. Also reuse FS-Researcher (`src/polaris_graph/retrieval/fs_researcher_query_gen.py`) and the outline agent. The generator already has `deliverable_spec` / `scope_spec` seams (`generate_multi_section_report`, ~line 3010) that the driver doesn't populate.
35	
36	## Champion pipeline map (task 72, scored 0.4447)
37	FS-Researcher retrieval (queries → fetch) → dedup/consolidate (cp3→cp4: 997 evidence rows / 919 URLs / 329 baskets / 55 same-work-groups) → outline agent (PG_OUTLINE_AGENT=1: ~9 react turns, searches ~100+ more URLs live) → parallel section writers → strict_verify (frozen; drops unverifiable) → assemble → report. Driver: `scripts/compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py` (uses a PRE-BUILT corpus — the real gate must run at retrieval, not on the frozen corpus).
38	
39	## Reference material (provided separately)
40	You will be given a **2026 best-practices research digest** on how frontier deep-research products implement prompt clarification + research-plan proposal + confirmation. Use it as a reference; adapt, don't copy.
41	
42	## Output format (markdown)
43	1. **The gate architecture** (diagram + stages), interactive AND autonomous modes.
44	2. **The contract schema** (full, typed).
45	3. **The understanding engine** (how it decomposes/clarifies/infers; prompt + output schema shape).
46	4. **Per-stage enforcement map** (retrieval → outline → compose → render → compliance audit), with `file:function` hook points in the champion code.
47	5. **Your recommendation on the gate ↔ outline-agent seam** (B).
48	6. **Ask-vs-assume policy** (C).
49	7. **Generality argument** (E) + risks/failure modes + what needs a spike.
50	Rank nothing; be complete. This feeds an Opus consolidation into one buildable design.
51

# DESIGN BRIEF — the Research Planning Gate (independent design review)

You are one of two independent designers (the other is {OTHER}). Do NOT coordinate. Produce a rigorous, buildable design. Cite `file:function` when you reference our code. Say "I don't know / needs a spike" rather than hand-wave.

## The goal
Design a **Research Planning Gate**: a front stage that runs BEFORE any expensive retrieval, deeply understands an arbitrary user prompt, and produces a structured, user-approvable research plan that then guardrails every downstream stage so the final report fits the prompt with **zero drift** — on ANY prompt, not just benchmark task 72.

This is the pattern ChatGPT Deep Research / Gemini Deep Research / Claude use: a small reasoning step that clarifies intent, proposes a research plan (direction + scope + outline), lets the user edit or approve, then runs. We want our own version.

```
User prompt
  → [GATE: understand deeply] decompose multiple folded-in questions; surface ambiguity;
      infer implicit intent (audience, purpose, depth); extract the full contract
  → [PROPOSE plan]: research direction + scope + outline + assumptions + clarifying questions
  → user EDITS (wrong) or APPROVES/Continue (right)
  → deep research runs; the approved plan is the guardrail for retrieval → outline → compose → render
```

## What you must DESIGN (deliverables)
1. **The understanding engine.** How the gate reads a prompt to produce: (a) decomposed sub-questions (a compound prompt → N research threads); (b) the full **contract** — every dimension a prompt can impose: source scope (type, quality bar, language, recency window, geography, domain, prohibited sources), content requirements (required topics/coverage, must-address questions, entities include/exclude, required comparisons, breadth vs depth), presentation (document type, structure, length, required sections, tables/figures), tone & audience, meta (citation style, hedging, POV); (c) ambiguity flags + implicit-intent inferences, each marked EXPLICIT vs INFERRED; (d) assumptions. Is it one LLM call or a small reasoning loop? Give the actual prompt/schema shape.
2. **The plan artifact** the user reviews/edits — its data model, what is editable, and how edits flow back into the contract.
3. **Per-stage guardrail map** — how each approved contract term is ENFORCED at: FS-Researcher/retrieval (scope → queries + ingestion filter), the outline agent (required coverage + approved outline), compose (tone/style), render (format/length/tables), and a final **compliance audit** (did the output satisfy every term — this is NOT faithfulness).

## The CRITICAL questions you MUST answer (these are where it breaks)
- **A. Benchmark has NO human.** DeepResearch Bench hands over a prompt and wants a report — nobody answers clarifications or clicks Continue. So the gate needs TWO modes: **(i) interactive** (real users: ask, show plan, edit, approve) and **(ii) autonomous** (benchmark / "run with best guess": infer the whole contract, make best assumptions, proceed WITHOUT asking). Design both. Without mode (ii) the gate cannot be RACE-scored. This is the single most important constraint.
- **B. Relationship to the EXISTING outline agent.** The champion already has an outline agent that "deeply thinks" and searches more evidence (`src/polaris_graph/outline/outline_agent.py`, `run_outline_agent_or_legacy`; the driver runs it via PG_OUTLINE_AGENT=1). Does the new gate REPLACE its planning, FEED it a locked contract + approved outline, or WRAP it? Design the exact seam so two planners don't fight. Recommend one.
- **C. When to ASK vs ASSUME.** Real products ask 1–3 questions max. Give the policy for "worth a clarifying question" vs "state an assumption the user can override." Operator rules: NEVER invent a constraint the user didn't state; prefer fresher sources when recency unspecified (soft, not a hard cutoff).
- **D. No starvation.** Scope must drive retrieval to GO FIND enough in-scope sources — never filter a fixed pool down. (We already proved filtering the frozen 997-row unscoped corpus down to 131 journal rows starves the report: RACE 0.4447 → 0.3264, and even Instruction-Following fell.) The scope contract must reach FS-Researcher's query generation.
- **E. Generality.** Validate the design against very different prompts: single vs multi-question, tightly-scoped vs wide-open, and across several of the 100 DRB tasks — not just task 72.

## Hard rails (non-negotiable)
- **FAITHFULNESS IS FROZEN.** The gate is entirely upstream; it must NEVER touch `strict_verify` / the citation verifier / the drop rule (`src/polaris_graph/generator/provenance_generator.py`). No new verification, no new faithfulness test.
- Priority: **COVERAGE > INSIGHT > READABILITY**.
- **Reuse, don't rebuild.** A validated constraint extractor (the "rule-reader") already exists on branch `round1-if-compiler @ f2b0d5e` (`src/polaris_graph/instruction/constraint_extractor.py`) — it correctly extracted journal-only/English/4 coverage topics/literature_review/academic tone from task 72. Reuse/extend it. Also reuse FS-Researcher (`src/polaris_graph/retrieval/fs_researcher_query_gen.py`) and the outline agent. The generator already has `deliverable_spec` / `scope_spec` seams (`generate_multi_section_report`, ~line 3010) that the driver doesn't populate.

## Champion pipeline map (task 72, scored 0.4447)
FS-Researcher retrieval (queries → fetch) → dedup/consolidate (cp3→cp4: 997 evidence rows / 919 URLs / 329 baskets / 55 same-work-groups) → outline agent (PG_OUTLINE_AGENT=1: ~9 react turns, searches ~100+ more URLs live) → parallel section writers → strict_verify (frozen; drops unverifiable) → assemble → report. Driver: `scripts/compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py` (uses a PRE-BUILT corpus — the real gate must run at retrieval, not on the frozen corpus).

## Reference material (provided separately)
You will be given a **2026 best-practices research digest** on how frontier deep-research products implement prompt clarification + research-plan proposal + confirmation. Use it as a reference; adapt, don't copy.

## Output format (markdown)
1. **The gate architecture** (diagram + stages), interactive AND autonomous modes.
2. **The contract schema** (full, typed).
3. **The understanding engine** (how it decomposes/clarifies/infers; prompt + output schema shape).
4. **Per-stage enforcement map** (retrieval → outline → compose → render → compliance audit), with `file:function` hook points in the champion code.
5. **Your recommendation on the gate ↔ outline-agent seam** (B).
6. **Ask-vs-assume policy** (C).
7. **Generality argument** (E) + risks/failure modes + what needs a spike.
Rank nothing; be complete. This feeds an Opus consolidation into one buildable design.

content.content:
1	# The Pre-Research Planning / Clarification Gate: A 2026 Best-Practices Digest
2	
3	How frontier "deep research" products understand a prompt, propose a plan, and confirm scope *before* spending money on retrieval.
4	
5	## 1. The clarification step: do they ask, how many, what triggers it?
6	Convergent pattern: a lightweight reasoning step runs before retrieval, and it either asks a small number of clarifying questions or silently rewrites the prompt into a brief.
7	- **ChatGPT Deep Research** asks clarifying questions in the UI ("may ask clarifying questions to confirm your goals, alongside a research plan"). Example: "Would you like a focus on B2B or B2C AI SaaS competitors? Should I prioritize review sites like TrustPilot or financial data like Crunchbase?" User can reply "use your best judgment." (help.openai.com/en/articles/10500283)
8	- **OpenAI Deep Research API** — reference multi-agent design has a **Clarifier Agent**: "Ask 2–3 clarifying questions… do not ask for information the user already provided." **2–3 is the canonical count.** (OpenAI Cookbook: deep_research_api_agents)
9	- **Gemini Deep Research** does NOT front-load questions; leads with an **editable research plan** revised in natural language. (blog.google / gemini.google/overview/deep-research)
10	- **Claude Research** trained to ask on ambiguous tasks; Claude Code "asks for clarification more than twice as often on the most complex tasks" — **uncertainty-calibrated asking**. (anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system)
11	- **Perplexity Deep Research** does NOT front-load; interprets the query and "formulates a detailed research plan," refining as it learns.
12	Trigger logic: a **Triage Agent** binary decision — "if context missing → clarify; else → research." LangChain Open Deep Research uses a `clarify_with_user` node with structured `need_clarification` boolean. Trigger is **missing essential attributes** (time frame, budget, audience, source type, acronyms) — NOT stylistic ambiguity.
13	
14	## 2. The research-plan artifact
15	Two styles: user-facing editable plan (Gemini, ChatGPT) vs internal research brief (API/OSS).
16	- **Gemini editable plan**: laid out before reading sources; user clicks "Edit plan" and redirects in natural language. Step-by-step sub-task breakdown; model later decides parallel vs sequential.
17	- **ChatGPT**: shows a plan the user "can review and edit… so the report stays aligned."
18	- **LangChain internal "research brief"**: clarification compressed into "a comprehensive, yet focused research brief… our north star," referenced throughout research + writing; captures known preferences, key dimensions, source preferences, output language; leaves unstated details open-ended.
19	- **GPT-Researcher**: plan = 4–6 focused sub-questions, each dispatched to a parallel agent.
20	Complete plan = sub-questions/decomposition, scope, source preferences, output format/language, explicit assumptions. Editable in consumer products; internal brief in API/OSS.
21	
22	## 3. The "understanding engine"
23	No leading system uses a single blind LLM call. Pattern = short reasoning step (or small multi-agent pipeline) → structured brief → decomposition.
24	- **OpenAI API pipeline**: Triage → Clarifier → Instruction (brief-builder) → Research. Instruction prompt directives: "Maximize Specificity and Detail," "Fill in Unstated But Necessary Dimensions as Open-Ended," "Avoid Unwarranted Assumptions," first-person framing, explicit format/language/source requests.
25	- **Anthropic extended-thinking planning**: lead agent "uses thinking to plan… assessing which tools fit, query complexity, subagent count, each subagent's role." Each subagent brief needs "objective, output format, tool/source guidance, clear task boundaries."
26	- **STORM (Stanford)**: perspective-guided question asking — "directly prompting the LM to ask questions does not work well," so it surveys similar articles to discover *perspectives*, then simulates writer↔expert conversations to build outline + questions before writing.
27	- **LangChain**: structured output for clarify decision (`need_clarification`, `question`, `verification`) + retry, then `write_research_brief` — separates "should I ask?" from "what's the plan?"
28	Recurring techniques: intent triage/classification, decomposition into 4–6 sub-questions, prompt-rewriting into structured brief, ReAct-style refinement.
29	
30	## 4. Autonomous vs interactive (THE sharp fork)
31	- **OpenAI Deep Research API deliberately SKIPS clarification.** For unattended runs: bolt on your own lightweight clarifier (gpt-4.1) OR skip to a **prompt-rewriting** step anticipating common cases (cost: verbosity, latency, tokens). Core models then autonomously plan sub-questions.
32	- **LangChain Open Deep Research**: clarification is **configurable — can be disabled** to run headless; brief generation still runs.
33	- **Anthropic Auto Mode**: "Auto mode is active… Execute immediately… Make reasonable assumptions and proceed on low-risk work," though "Claude still asks when your prompt or a skill explicitly relies on it." Safety via classifier approval, not human prompts.
34	**Best practice for autonomous:** replace the ask-gate with a **rewrite-gate** — make assumptions open-ended + explicit, never invent specifics, surface them in the output.
35	
36	## 5. Scope/constraint handling
37	Constraints captured INTO the brief during planning, then enforced downstream by making the brief the persistent "north star."
38	- OpenAI Instruction Builder: specify output language + source preferences; request structured formats (tables/headers).
39	- LangChain research-topic prompt: source preferences ("official > aggregators, original papers > secondary summaries"); preserves all known preferences + dimensions.
40	- Anthropic subagent briefs carry output format + source/tool guidance + boundaries.
41	- GPT-Researcher: SourceCurator ranks by credibility; ContextManager filters by embedding similarity — enforcement moved downstream into retrieval agents.
42	Key move: constraints written into a **durable brief object** every downstream agent reads — not left only in the prompt.
43	
44	## 6. When to ask vs assume — 2026 consensus
45	Calibration, not fixed policy; framed as a decision problem under uncertainty.
46	- Formalized: "ask a clarification question exactly when expected regret exceeds a cost threshold" (arXiv 2603.26233 Ask or Assume?; arXiv 2511.08798). Ask only when uncertainty high AND cost of being wrong high.
47	- Anthropic: asking scales with complexity (2× on complex tasks) = calibration.
48	- **Cap questions at 2–3**; never re-ask what's answered ("if you already asked a clarifying question, you almost always do not need another").
49	- UX: avoid over-asking; clarifying questions are a fallback/recovery layer, not a mandatory opener.
50	- Counter-risk (drift) is real: direct execution when confident; clarify when capabilities insufficient / misunderstanding likely.
51	Bottom line: ask ≤3 questions, only about *decision-changing* unknowns, only when confidence low; otherwise rewrite with open-ended assumptions and proceed.
52	
53	## 7. Failure modes / lessons
54	- **Over-provisioning from bad planning** (Anthropic: "50 subagents for simple queries") — fix: scale effort to complexity.
55	- **Vague sub-briefs → duplicated work** — fix: specific objective + output format + boundaries per subagent.
56	- **Early commitments high-leverage + hard to reverse**: planning errors low-frequency but consequential; "premature candidate introduction" narrows search space (arXiv 2606.02060).
57	- **Goal/scope drift** now a measured failure mode; scope creep + data-quality ≈ 61% of agent failures in production.
58	- **Latency/token cost of over-clarifying** (OpenAI: broad rewrite → verbosity, latency, tokens).
59	- **Source bias baked in at planning** (agents chose SEO content farms over authoritative sources) — source prefs must be explicit in the brief.
60	
61	## Design takeaways for OUR gate
62	1. Make the gate a cheap, separate step before retrieval (triage → clarify/rewrite → brief → research) with a small/fast model.
63	2. Cap clarifying questions at 2–3; only *decision-changing* unknowns (time frame, audience, source type, budget, acronyms) — never style.
64	3. Gate on calibrated uncertainty × cost of error, not a fixed rule; scale asking with complexity.
65	4. Always emit a structured brief artifact (sub-questions, scope, source/recency/language constraints, output format, explicit assumptions) = persistent north star every stage reads.
66	5. Decompose into ~4–6 sub-questions with per-question objective/format/source guidance/boundaries.
67	6. Editable plan in interactive mode; internal brief in headless mode.
68	7. Ship an explicit autonomous/"auto" mode: swap ask-gate for rewrite-gate — fill unstated dimensions open-ended, never invent, surface assumptions in output.
69	8. Never re-ask what's answered — feed history into the clarify decision.
70	9. Scale effort to complexity at the gate.
71	10. Encode source-quality preferences explicitly (official > aggregators, primary > secondary, recency windows).
72	11. Treat early commitments as high-risk: keep the plan revisable; avoid premature lock-in; let the research loop reopen scope on conflicting evidence.
73	12. Instrument the gate for over-asking + latency.
74	
75	Primary sources: anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system · anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-auto-mode · OpenAI Cookbook deep_research_api (+ _agents) · help.openai.com/en/articles/10500283 · gemini.google/overview/deep-research + blog.google · langchain.com/blog/open-deep-research · DeepWiki gpt-researcher · storm-project.stanford.edu · arXiv 2606.02060, 2603.26233, 2511.08798.
76

file.content:
# The Pre-Research Planning / Clarification Gate: A 2026 Best-Practices Digest

How frontier "deep research" products understand a prompt, propose a plan, and confirm scope *before* spending money on retrieval.

## 1. The clarification step: do they ask, how many, what triggers it?
Convergent pattern: a lightweight reasoning step runs before retrieval, and it either asks a small number of clarifying questions or silently rewrites the prompt into a brief.
- **ChatGPT Deep Research** asks clarifying questions in the UI ("may ask clarifying questions to confirm your goals, alongside a research plan"). Example: "Would you like a focus on B2B or B2C AI SaaS competitors? Should I prioritize review sites like TrustPilot or financial data like Crunchbase?" User can reply "use your best judgment." (help.openai.com/en/articles/10500283)
- **OpenAI Deep Research API** — reference multi-agent design has a **Clarifier Agent**: "Ask 2–3 clarifying questions… do not ask for information the user already provided." **2–3 is the canonical count.** (OpenAI Cookbook: deep_research_api_agents)
- **Gemini Deep Research** does NOT front-load questions; leads with an **editable research plan** revised in natural language. (blog.google / gemini.google/overview/deep-research)
- **Claude Research** trained to ask on ambiguous tasks; Claude Code "asks for clarification more than twice as often on the most complex tasks" — **uncertainty-calibrated asking**. (anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system)
- **Perplexity Deep Research** does NOT front-load; interprets the query and "formulates a detailed research plan," refining as it learns.
Trigger logic: a **Triage Agent** binary decision — "if context missing → clarify; else → research." LangChain Open Deep Research uses a `clarify_with_user` node with structured `need_clarification` boolean. Trigger is **missing essential attributes** (time frame, budget, audience, source type, acronyms) — NOT stylistic ambiguity.

## 2. The research-plan artifact
Two styles: user-facing editable plan (Gemini, ChatGPT) vs internal research brief (API/OSS).
- **Gemini editable plan**: laid out before reading sources; user clicks "Edit plan" and redirects in natural language. Step-by-step sub-task breakdown; model later decides parallel vs sequential.
- **ChatGPT**: shows a plan the user "can review and edit… so the report stays aligned."
- **LangChain internal "research brief"**: clarification compressed into "a comprehensive, yet focused research brief… our north star," referenced throughout research + writing; captures known preferences, key dimensions, source preferences, output language; leaves unstated details open-ended.
- **GPT-Researcher**: plan = 4–6 focused sub-questions, each dispatched to a parallel agent.
Complete plan = sub-questions/decomposition, scope, source preferences, output format/language, explicit assumptions. Editable in consumer products; internal brief in API/OSS.

## 3. The "understanding engine"
No leading system uses a single blind LLM call. Pattern = short reasoning step (or small multi-agent pipeline) → structured brief → decomposition.
- **OpenAI API pipeline**: Triage → Clarifier → Instruction (brief-builder) → Research. Instruction prompt directives: "Maximize Specificity and Detail," "Fill in Unstated But Necessary Dimensions as Open-Ended," "Avoid Unwarranted Assumptions," first-person framing, explicit format/language/source requests.
- **Anthropic extended-thinking planning**: lead agent "uses thinking to plan… assessing which tools fit, query complexity, subagent count, each subagent's role." Each subagent brief needs "objective, output format, tool/source guidance, clear task boundaries."
- **STORM (Stanford)**: perspective-guided question asking — "directly prompting the LM to ask questions does not work well," so it surveys similar articles to discover *perspectives*, then simulates writer↔expert conversations to build outline + questions before writing.
- **LangChain**: structured output for clarify decision (`need_clarification`, `question`, `verification`) + retry, then `write_research_brief` — separates "should I ask?" from "what's the plan?"
Recurring techniques: intent triage/classification, decomposition into 4–6 sub-questions, prompt-rewriting into structured brief, ReAct-style refinement.

## 4. Autonomous vs interactive (THE sharp fork)
- **OpenAI Deep Research API deliberately SKIPS clarification.** For unattended runs: bolt on your own lightweight clarifier (gpt-4.1) OR skip to a **prompt-rewriting** step anticipating common cases (cost: verbosity, latency, tokens). Core models then autonomously plan sub-questions.
- **LangChain Open Deep Research**: clarification is **configurable — can be disabled** to run headless; brief generation still runs.
- **Anthropic Auto Mode**: "Auto mode is active… Execute immediately… Make reasonable assumptions and proceed on low-risk work," though "Claude still asks when your prompt or a skill explicitly relies on it." Safety via classifier approval, not human prompts.
**Best practice for autonomous:** replace the ask-gate with a **rewrite-gate** — make assumptions open-ended + explicit, never invent specifics, surface them in the output.

## 5. Scope/constraint handling
Constraints captured INTO the brief during planning, then enforced downstream by making the brief the persistent "north star."
- OpenAI Instruction Builder: specify output language + source preferences; request structured formats (tables/headers).
- LangChain research-topic prompt: source preferences ("official > aggregators, original papers > secondary summaries"); preserves all known preferences + dimensions.
- Anthropic subagent briefs carry output format + source/tool guidance + boundaries.
- GPT-Researcher: SourceCurator ranks by credibility; ContextManager filters by embedding similarity — enforcement moved downstream into retrieval agents.
Key move: constraints written into a **durable brief object** every downstream agent reads — not left only in the prompt.

## 6. When to ask vs assume — 2026 consensus
Calibration, not fixed policy; framed as a decision problem under uncertainty.
- Formalized: "ask a clarification question exactly when expected regret exceeds a cost threshold" (arXiv 2603.26233 Ask or Assume?; arXiv 2511.08798). Ask only when uncertainty high AND cost of being wrong high.
- Anthropic: asking scales with complexity (2× on complex tasks) = calibration.
- **Cap questions at 2–3**; never re-ask what's answered ("if you already asked a clarifying question, you almost always do not need another").
- UX: avoid over-asking; clarifying questions are a fallback/recovery layer, not a mandatory opener.
- Counter-risk (drift) is real: direct execution when confident; clarify when capabilities insufficient / misunderstanding likely.
Bottom line: ask ≤3 questions, only about *decision-changing* unknowns, only when confidence low; otherwise rewrite with open-ended assumptions and proceed.

## 7. Failure modes / lessons
- **Over-provisioning from bad planning** (Anthropic: "50 subagents for simple queries") — fix: scale effort to complexity.
- **Vague sub-briefs → duplicated work** — fix: specific objective + output format + boundaries per subagent.
- **Early commitments high-leverage + hard to reverse**: planning errors low-frequency but consequential; "premature candidate introduction" narrows search space (arXiv 2606.02060).
- **Goal/scope drift** now a measured failure mode; scope creep + data-quality ≈ 61% of agent failures in production.
- **Latency/token cost of over-clarifying** (OpenAI: broad rewrite → verbosity, latency, tokens).
- **Source bias baked in at planning** (agents chose SEO content farms over authoritative sources) — source prefs must be explicit in the brief.

## Design takeaways for OUR gate
1. Make the gate a cheap, separate step before retrieval (triage → clarify/rewrite → brief → research) with a small/fast model.
2. Cap clarifying questions at 2–3; only *decision-changing* unknowns (time frame, audience, source type, budget, acronyms) — never style.
3. Gate on calibrated uncertainty × cost of error, not a fixed rule; scale asking with complexity.
4. Always emit a structured brief artifact (sub-questions, scope, source/recency/language constraints, output format, explicit assumptions) = persistent north star every stage reads.
5. Decompose into ~4–6 sub-questions with per-question objective/format/source guidance/boundaries.
6. Editable plan in interactive mode; internal brief in headless mode.
7. Ship an explicit autonomous/"auto" mode: swap ask-gate for rewrite-gate — fill unstated dimensions open-ended, never invent, surface assumptions in output.
8. Never re-ask what's answered — feed history into the clarify decision.
9. Scale effort to complexity at the gate.
10. Encode source-quality preferences explicitly (official > aggregators, primary > secondary, recency windows).
11. Treat early commitments as high-risk: keep the plan revisable; avoid premature lock-in; let the research loop reopen scope on conflicting evidence.
12. Instrument the gate for over-asking + latency.

Primary sources: anthropic.com/engineering/multi-agent-research-system · anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-auto-mode · OpenAI Cookbook deep_research_api (+ _agents) · help.openai.com/en/articles/10500283 · gemini.google/overview/deep-research + blog.google · langchain.com/blog/open-deep-research · DeepWiki gpt-researcher · storm-project.stanford.edu · arXiv 2606.02060, 2603.26233, 2511.08798.

bot/outline-agent-box
  1140 src/polaris_graph/retrieval/fs_researcher_query_gen.py
  2507 src/polaris_graph/outline/outline_agent.py
   398 scripts/compose_agentic_report_s3gear329.py
 12083 src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py
 16128 total

<system-reminder>[Truncated: PARTIAL view — showing lines 1-918 of 1141 total (26402 tokens, cap 25000). Call Read with offset=919 limit=918 for the next page, or Grep to find a specific section. Do NOT answer from this page alone if the answer may be further in the file.]</system-reminder>

1	"""FS-Researcher adaptive query generation for the production retrieval path.
2	
3	I-recency-001 (#1296). FS-Researcher (arXiv 2602.01566) WON the recency-completion query-gen
4	re-bake-off under a positive-control-validated judge — balanced across axes (general drb_72
5	finding_coverage 0.561, clinical 3-slug avg 0.351), 2nd on BOTH and never weak. It SUPERSEDES
6	IterResearch, whose earlier "0.386" win did NOT reproduce on the validated judge (it scored 0.000
7	general / ~0.232 clinical = near-WORST). This wires FS-Researcher's PORTABLE scaffold into the
8	production sweep as the query generator, FLAG-GATED (PG_QGEN_FS_RESEARCHER; default OFF =>
9	byte-identical to the legacy template-facet path).
10	
11	The method (arXiv 2602.01566, primary-source verified): build an index.md table-of-contents by
12	deconstructing the question into sub-topics (a todo queue); for each todo derive ONE search query,
13	retrieve, and fold the result in; then run a FIXED 6-item self-review checklist (exhaustive
14	coverage: 'a question the KB cannot fully answer?'; information density: 'an aspect with only 1-2
15	weak sources?') whose output becomes the next round's deficient todos. Repeat until the checklist
16	reports NONE or the query budget is exhausted.
17	
18	FAITHFULNESS: this changes ONLY which queries are issued. Every query still flows through the
19	UNCHANGED `run_live_retrieval` (scope gate, tier classify, fetch, provenance), and the faithfulness
20	engine (strict_verify / NLI / 4-role / provenance) is never touched. The per-query retrieval
21	results are MERGED (dedup by source URL, evidence ids renumbered globally) into one
22	LiveRetrievalResult so downstream (consolidation -> generation -> verify -> render) sees the same
23	contract as today. Mirrors `iterresearch_query_gen.py` (`merge_retrieval_results` is identical).
24	"""
25	
26	from __future__ import annotations
27	
28	import logging
29	import os
30	import re
31	import time
32	from typing import Any, Callable
33	
34	logger = logging.getLogger("polaris_graph.fs_researcher_query_gen")
35	
36	# (research_question, **kw) -> LiveRetrievalResult. Injected so this module never imports the
37	# 1000-line live_retriever at module load (and so it is unit-testable on a stub).
38	PerQueryRetrieveFn = Callable[..., Any]
39	# (prompt) -> text. The GLM-5.2 policy. Injected (async client wrapped to sync by the caller).
40	LlmFn = Callable[[str], str]
41	
42	
43	def fs_researcher_enabled() -> bool:
44	    """True iff the FS-Researcher query-gen path is flag-enabled (default OFF = legacy behaviour)."""
45	    return os.getenv("PG_QGEN_FS_RESEARCHER", "0").strip() in ("1", "true", "True")
46	
47	
48	def _max_queries() -> int:
49	    """Max search queries issued (the equal-budget cap the bake-off used). Caps cost."""
50	    return int(os.getenv("PG_QGEN_FS_RESEARCHER_MAX_QUERIES", "35"))
51	
52	
53	def _max_rounds() -> int:
54	    """Max outer todo-queue / checklist re-plan rounds."""
55	    return int(os.getenv("PG_QGEN_FS_RESEARCHER_MAX_ROUNDS", "6"))
56	
57	
58	def _qgen_parallel_workers() -> int:
59	    """I-deepfix-001 Wave-3 (#1344): bounded-parallel worker count for the seed-frontier fan-out.
60	
61	    Default 1 = SERIAL, byte-identical to the legacy per-query loop. >1 = a bounded ThreadPool over
62	    ``per_query_retrieve`` with an ORDER-STABLE merge. A compute-safety UP bound (LAW VI env cap
63	    ``PG_QGEN_PARALLEL_QUERIES``), never a breadth target — the SAME query set is issued, only
64	    concurrently. Fixes the measured throughput collapse where a serial per-query retrieval tail hit
65	    the retrieval wall and only ~3 of ~35 planned queries fired. §-1.3: additive orchestration; the
66	    frozen faithfulness engine is untouched. A bad value falls back to 1 (serial)."""
67	    try:
68	        return max(1, int(os.getenv("PG_QGEN_PARALLEL_QUERIES", "1")))
69	    except ValueError:
70	        return 1
71	
72	
73	def _issue_seed_frontier(
74	    seed_queries: list[str],
75	    seen_q: set[str],
76	    budget: int,
77	    per_query_retrieve: PerQueryRetrieveFn,
78	    retrieve_kwargs: dict,
79	    wall_passed: Callable[[], bool],
80	) -> tuple[list[str], list[Any], list[Any]]:
81	    """Issue up to ``budget`` unique, not-yet-seen seed queries (first-seen-wins, in seed order)
82	    through ``per_query_retrieve`` and return ``(issued_queries, results, corpus_rows)`` in seed order.
83	
84	    ``seen_q`` is updated IN PLACE with exactly the issued queries. Serial + byte-identical to the
85	    legacy loop at ``PG_QGEN_PARALLEL_QUERIES=1``; bounded-parallel (order-stable merge) when >1.
86	
87	    I-deepfix-001 Wave-3 (#1344): the parallel path selects the SAME deduped, budget-capped query set
88	    the serial scan would issue, dispatches them concurrently in a bounded ThreadPool, and merges the
89	    results back in seed order — so the issued query set + result order are identical to serial; only
90	    the wall-timing of a mid-frontier trip differs (the parallel dispatch issues the whole batch under
91	    one wall instead of dropping the tail to a serial-loop wall). §-1.3 additive: SAME queries, SAME
92	    ``per_query_retrieve`` chokepoint, SAME per-query result contract; drops zero sources; the frozen
93	    faithfulness engine is untouched.
94	    """
95	    issued: list[str] = []
96	    results: list[Any] = []
97	    corpus_rows: list[Any] = []
98	
99	    workers = _qgen_parallel_workers()
100	    if workers <= 1:
101	        # SERIAL — byte-identical to the legacy per-query loop (wall + budget checked per iteration).
102	        for q in seed_queries:
103	            if len(issued) >= budget or wall_passed():
104	                break
105	            k = q.lower()
106	            if k in seen_q:
107	                continue
108	            seen_q.add(k)
109	            issued.append(q)
110	            result = per_query_retrieve(research_question=q, **retrieve_kwargs)
111	            results.append(result)
112	            corpus_rows.extend(list(getattr(result, "evidence_rows", None) or []))
113	        return issued, results, corpus_rows
114	
115	    # PARALLEL — order-stable. Select the IDENTICAL query set the serial scan would issue (dedup +
116	    # budget), then issue concurrently and merge in seed order. The anti-dark fire marker is emitted at
117	    # EVERY return path below carrying the REALIZED ``issued`` count — I-deepfix-001 Wave-3b (#1344,
118	    # Codex P1.2): the prior single PRE-ISSUE marker logged ``selected=N`` BEFORE ``selected[0]`` was
119	    # issued, so a wall trip on ``selected[0]`` skipped ``_rest`` yet the log still read "fired N"
120	    # (INTENT, not realized ISSUE). ``selected`` is declared up-front so the marker is valid on the
121	    # wall-passed early return too.
122	    selected: list[str] = []
123	
124	    def _emit_fanout_marker() -> None:
125	        # I-deepfix-001 Wave-3b (#1344): anti-dark liveness marker (Fable/Codex P1) — REALIZED-count
126	        # edition. Emitted ONLY on the >1 (parallel) path so the serial default stays byte-identical AND
127	        # the official run's log can DISTINGUISH serial from parallel-N — the exact seam where the "only
128	        # ~3 of ~35 queries fired" collapse was diagnosed. ``selected=`` is the pre-issue dedup count
129	        # (intent breadcrumb); ``issued=`` is the REALIZED fan-out the canary keys on, so a wall-tripped
130	        # 1-of-N now logs issued=1 (NOT selected=N). issued=0 (wall tripped before any seed /
131	        # all-duplicate / budget-0) is the eligible-yet-zero signal — NEVER gated on >0 (§-1.3: a wall
132	        # truncation is compute-safety, not a source drop). Structural presence + counts; the frozen
133	        # faithfulness engine is untouched.
134	        logger.info(
135	            "[activation] qgen_parallel_fanout: workers=%d selected=%d issued=%d",
136	            workers, len(selected), len(issued),
137	        )
138	
139	    if wall_passed():
140	        _emit_fanout_marker()
141	        return issued, results, corpus_rows
142	    # I-deepfix-001 Wave-3 (#1344) — Codex P0 (no-drop / order-stable): dedup the SELECTION against a
143	    # LOCAL key set and mutate the shared ``seen_q`` ONLY as each query is actually ISSUED (mirroring
144	    # the serial loop, which adds to ``seen_q`` immediately before it retrieves). The prior code marked
145	    # EVERY ``selected`` query seen up-front; when the retrieval wall tripped on ``selected[0]`` the
146	    # un-issued ``_rest`` were skipped BUT stayed in ``seen_q``, so the downstream facet-completeness
147	    # expansion loop (which reads ``seen_q`` as ``already_issued``) could never re-issue them — a real
148	    # dropped-source breadth loss. Marking seen only on issue leaves the un-issued tail eligible for
149	    # re-issue, exactly as the serial path leaves it (§-1.3 no drop).
150	    _selected_keys: set[str] = set()
151	    for q in seed_queries:
152	        if len(selected) >= budget:
153	            break
154	        k = q.lower()
155	        if k in seen_q or k in _selected_keys:
156	            continue
157	        _selected_keys.add(k)
158	        selected.append(q)
159	    if not selected:
160	        _emit_fanout_marker()  # realized issued=0 (all-duplicate / budget-0): eligible-yet-zero (§-1.3)
161	        return issued, results, corpus_rows
162	    from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
163	
164	    def _one(q: str) -> Any:
165	        return per_query_retrieve(research_question=q, **retrieve_kwargs)
166	
167	    # RACE-SAFETY at the PRODUCTION call site (Fable P1): the injected ``per_query_retrieve`` closure
168	    # (``_iter_per_query_retrieve`` in run_honest_sweep_r3) attaches the layer-4 DOI ``seed_urls`` to
169	    # the FIRST call it sees via a check-then-set on shared state written for SERIAL calls. Under a
170	    # concurrent first batch several threads would each read ``done=False`` and re-fetch the SAME seed
171	    # PDFs — never DROPS a source (additive-safe, faithfulness-neutral) but burns paid fetches
172	    # nondeterministically and which query carries the seed becomes random. Bind the seed
173	    # DETERMINISTICALLY to the FIRST selected query by issuing it SERIALLY before the fan-out; the
174	    # remaining queries then dispatch in the bounded pool. The parallel path now attaches the seed to
175	    # exactly the query a serial run would (order-stable) — a strict throughput win, no shared-state race.
176	    _first_result = _one(selected[0])
177	    seen_q.add(selected[0].lower())  # Codex P0: mark seen ONLY on actual issue (like the serial loop)
178	    issued.append(selected[0])
179	    results.append(_first_result)
180	    corpus_rows.extend(list(getattr(_first_result, "evidence_rows", None) or []))
181	    _rest = selected[1:]
182	    # I-deepfix-001 Wave-3 (#1344) — retrieval-wall re-check (Codex P1): ``selected[0]`` above is
183	    # issued SERIALLY and can itself consume the shared retrieval wall. Mirror the serial loop's
184	    # per-iteration ``wall_passed()`` guard by re-checking BEFORE dispatching the remaining frontier;
185	    # otherwise a wall tripped by ``selected[0]`` would still launch the whole ``_rest`` batch past the
186	    # wall — violating the stated retrieval-wall bound (off-path execution/spend). Skipping ``_rest`` on
187	    # a passed wall is EXACTLY what the serial path does (its next iteration would ``break``), so this
188	    # never issues MORE than serial and coverage stays additive — the wall is the compute-safety bound,
189	    # not a source drop (§-1.3). OFF (workers<=1) is untouched: this branch is parallel-only.
190	    if _rest and not wall_passed():
191	        with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=min(workers, len(_rest))) as _ex:
192	            _result_list = list(_ex.map(_one, _rest))
193	        for q, result in zip(_rest, _result_list):
194	            seen_q.add(q.lower())  # Codex P0: mark seen ONLY on actual issue (like the serial loop)
195	            issued.append(q)
196	            results.append(result)
197	            corpus_rows.extend(list(getattr(result, "evidence_rows", None) or []))
198	    _emit_fanout_marker()  # realized issued count (serial-first + parallel-rest, or wall-tripped 1-of-N)
199	    return issued, results, corpus_rows
200	
201	
202	def _seed_angles_per_facet() -> int:
203	    """R2 (I-deepfix-001, #1344): how many PRIMARY angle queries per facet the SEED pass
204	    issues when the R2 facet-completeness expansion loop is ON.
205	
206	    The seed pass issues the first ``_seed_angles_per_facet()`` angle(s) of every facet
207	    (breadth-first across ALL facets) and RESERVES each facet's remaining angle queries for
208	    the R2 completeness loop to fire ONLY on the facets the seed corpus leaves UNCOVERED.
209	    This is what makes the expansion loop non-vacuous: the prior code registered EVERY angle
210	    query as a seed, so :func:`facet_completeness.run_facet_expansion` (which draws from the
211	    SAME ``facet.queries``) always read "frontier exhausted" and issued zero expansion
212	    queries (the Codex/Fable P1).
213	
214	    A compute-safety split (seed breadth first, then deepen only the gaps) — never a breadth
215	    target (§-1.3). Default 2. When R2 is OFF the full angle frontier is seeded (no reserve
216	    carve), so the R1-only path is unchanged in query count."""
217	    try:
218	        return max(1, int(os.getenv("PG_EXPERT_FACET_SEED_ANGLES", "2")))
219	    except ValueError:
220	        return 2
221	
222	
223	def _breadth_first_seeds(facets: list[Any], angle_limit: "int | None") -> list[str]:
224	    """Order the facets' angle queries BREADTH-FIRST (angle-major): every facet's angle-0
225	    before any facet's angle-1, and so on.
226	
227	    Spreads the seed query budget across ALL facets first — fixing the measured "only 7 of
228	    35 facets seeded" starvation where a facet-major seed drained the whole budget on the
229	    first few facets and later facets were never queried. When ``angle_limit`` is set (R2
230	    on), only the first ``angle_limit`` angle(s) per facet are seeded so each facet's
231	    remaining angles stay a RESERVE for the R2 expansion loop; ``angle_limit=None`` (R2 off)
232	    seeds the full angle frontier so the R1-only path is unchanged in count. De-duplicates
233	    case-insensitively, order-preserving. Drops ZERO facets (§-1.3 — this only orders and
234	    splits the same query set)."""
235	    max_a = max((len(getattr(f, "queries", []) or []) for f in facets), default=0)
236	    if max_a <= 0:
237	        return []
238	    # Leave >= 1 reserve angle per facet when R2 is on (clamp so a small angle count can
239	    # never accidentally seed every angle and re-starve the expansion loop).
240	    if angle_limit is None:
241	        limit = max_a
242	    else:
243	        limit = min(max(1, angle_limit), max(1, max_a - 1))
244	    seeds: list[str] = []
245	    seen: set[str] = set()
246	    for a in range(min(limit, max_a)):
247	        for f in facets:
248	            qs = getattr(f, "queries", []) or []
249	            if a < len(qs):
250	                q = qs[a]
251	                k = q.lower()
252	                if k not in seen:
253	                    seen.add(k)
254	                    seeds.append(q)
255	    return seeds
256	
257	
258	# R5 (I-deepfix-001, #1344): display names for the target languages, for the
259	# production translator prompt. Code -> human name so the LLM translates reliably.
260	_LANG_DISPLAY: dict[str, str] = {
261	    "zh": "Chinese", "ja": "Japanese", "ko": "Korean", "ru": "Russian",
262	    "ar": "Arabic", "fr": "French", "de": "German", "es": "Spanish",
263	}
264	
265	
266	def _multilingual_native_reserve() -> int:
267	    """R5 (I-deepfix-001, #1344): the minimum number of native-language queries
268	    GUARANTEED a slot within the FS-Researcher query budget on a multilingual task.
269	
270	    Without this, the multilingual additions are appended AFTER every English seed,
271	    so a wide English R1 frontier (e.g. 12 facets x 5 angles = 60 seeds) fills the
272	    whole ``PG_QGEN_FS_RESEARCHER_MAX_QUERIES`` budget and NO native-language query
273	    is ever issued — the corpus stays English-only on a zh task (the Codex/Fable P1).
274	
275	    This is a language-ROUTING guarantee (ensure the task's OWN language is actually
276	    queried), NOT a breadth target (§-1.3): it reorders the SAME query set so a
277	    bounded slice of native queries lands inside the budget; it adds no query and
278	    drops none. It is clamped so it can never displace more than half the English
279	    breadth. Env-driven `PG_MULTILINGUAL_QUERY_RESERVE` (default 6)."""
280	    try:
281	        return max(0, int(os.getenv("PG_MULTILINGUAL_QUERY_RESERVE", "6")))
282	    except ValueError:
283	        return 6
284	
285	
286	def _make_multilingual_translate_fn(
287	    llm: LlmFn,
288	    base_queries: list[str],
289	    non_english_langs: tuple[str, ...],
290	) -> "Callable[[str, str], str] | None":
291	    """Build a bounded, memoized ``(english_query, lang) -> translated_query`` using
292	    the injected GLM policy, so an EXPLICIT-language task ('Answer in Chinese') with
293	    an all-ASCII English body actually emits native-language queries on the production
294	    path (the Codex P1). The question carries no native script there, so a true
295	    translation is the ONLY source of a native query.
296	
297	    Cost is bounded to ONE ``llm`` call per target language (the whole seed batch is
298	    translated in a single call, not one call per query), memoized. Best-effort: any
299	    exception or empty / echo reply yields no translation for that query, and the
300	    module simply skips it — the faithfulness engine is never touched (this only
301	    decides WHICH queries are searched). Returns ``None`` when translation is
302	    unavailable (no ``llm`` / no seed queries / no target language)."""
303	    if llm is None or not base_queries or not non_english_langs:
304	        return None
305	    _cache: dict[str, dict[str, str]] = {}
306	
307	    def _batch(lang: str) -> dict[str, str]:
308	        if lang in _cache:
309	            return _cache[lang]
310	        mapping: dict[str, str] = {}
311	        try:
312	            numbered = "\n".join(f"{i + 1}. {q}" for i, q in enumerate(base_queries))
313	            lang_name = _LANG_DISPLAY.get(lang, lang)
314	            prompt = (
315	                f"Translate each of these web-search queries into {lang_name}. "
316	                "Output ONE translation per line, numbered to match the input, "
317	                "the translation only with no commentary or transliteration.\n\n"
318	                f"{numbered}"
319	            )
320	            reply = llm(prompt) or ""
321	            lines = _lines(reply, cap=len(base_queries) + 2)
322	            for eng, translated in zip(base_queries, lines):
323	                t = " ".join((translated or "").split()).strip()
324	                if t and t.lower() != (eng or "").strip().lower():
325	                    mapping[(eng or "").strip().lower()] = t
326	        except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — translation is best-effort, never fatal
327	            mapping = {}
328	        _cache[lang] = mapping
329	        return mapping
330	
331	    def _translate(query: str, lang: str) -> str:
332	        return _batch(lang).get((query or "").strip().lower(), "")
333	
334	    return _translate
335	
336	
337	def _reserve_native_within_budget(
338	    english_seeds: list[str],
339	    native_additions: list[str],
340	    max_queries: int,
341	) -> list[str]:
342	    """Reorder the seed queries so a bounded slice of native-language queries is
343	    GUARANTEED to fall inside the first ``max_queries`` issued positions.
344	
345	    The R5 expansion appends native queries after every English seed; the seed issue
346	    loop stops at ``max_queries``; so a wide English frontier starves the native
347	    queries out entirely (Codex/Fable P1). This reorders — never drops — the SAME
348	    query set: it keeps an English-breadth head, then the reserved native queries
349	    (so they issue within budget), then the English tail and any leftover native
350	    queries (issued only if budget remains). English-only tasks (empty
351	    ``native_additions``) are byte-identical (returns ``english_seeds`` unchanged).
352	
353	    §-1.3: a language-routing reorder, not a cap/target — every query is preserved;
354	    only issue ORDER changes so the task's own language is actually queried."""
355	    if not native_additions:
356	        return list(english_seeds)
357	    # Never displace more than half the English breadth (both dimensions survive).
358	    reserve = min(len(native_additions), _multilingual_native_reserve(), max(1, max_queries // 2))
359	    if reserve <= 0:
360	        return list(english_seeds) + list(native_additions)
361	    reserved_native = native_additions[:reserve]
362	    leftover_native = native_additions[reserve:]
363	    head_len = max(0, max_queries - reserve)
364	    english_head = english_seeds[:head_len]
365	    english_tail = english_seeds[head_len:]
366	    return english_head + reserved_native + english_tail + leftover_native
367

369	def _scope_anchored() -> bool:
370	    """I-deepfix-001 (#1344): True iff sub-query generation anchors each derived query to the
371	    ORIGINAL research question's scope. Default ON.
372	
373	    Fixes the observed drift (drb_72 v2): a bare sub-topic like 'manufacturing and supply chain
374	    automation' was searched WITHOUT the question's 'AI + labor market' framing, so the search
375	    generalised into the sub-topic's broad field (industrial-automation engineering) and pulled
376	    ~500 off-topic + predatory-journal results into the corpus. Carrying the research question
377	    into both the TOC-deconstruction and the per-todo query-derivation keeps every sub-query
378	    on-subject.
379	
380	    Faithfulness-neutral: this changes ONLY which sources are SEARCHED (more on-topic); it does
381	    not touch tiering, verification, citation, or any faithfulness gate, and drops ZERO fetched
382	    sources (§-1.3 — this is retrieval SCOPING of the query, not a filter/cap on results).
383	    OFF (``0``/``false``) => byte-identical legacy prompts."""
384	    return os.getenv("PG_FS_RESEARCHER_SCOPE_ANCHOR", "1").strip() not in ("0", "false", "False")
385

388	    """I-deepfix-001 Wave-3 (#1344): env gate for the in-window landmark-study expander, read WITHOUT
389	    importing ``landmark_study_expander`` so a flag-OFF run NEVER imports that module — the expert-facet
390	    qgen path is byte-identical even if the module were absent/broken (Codex/Fable Wave-3 P0
391	    defense-in-depth: a commit assembled from the diff alone must not ModuleNotFoundError the OFF path).
392	    Mirrors ``landmark_study_expander.landmark_study_expansion_enabled`` exactly (default OFF; accepts
393	    ``1``/``true``/``on``/``yes``)."""

397	# GENERAL stance / view-diversification frames. Each is a short, TOPIC-AGNOSTIC lens phrase appended to
398	# a planned facet + the question's own scope anchor, so a good report on ANY contested topic is searched
399	# from multiple viewpoints (supporting / opposing / challenges / opportunities). This is a GENERAL
400	# deep-research technique — NO benchmark study title, country, or topic is baked in; the lens words are
401	# generic stance vocabulary only. The tuple is a FIXED small template set (not a tunable cap/target): the
402	# lane introduces ZERO new cap/threshold — its size is intrinsic to these four generic frames.
403	_STANCE_FRAMES: tuple[tuple[str, str], ...] = (
404	    ("support", "benefits positive evidence supporting arguments"),
405	    ("oppose", "risks criticism negative evidence opposing arguments"),
406	    ("challenge", "challenges limitations obstacles concerns"),
407	    ("opportunity", "opportunities mitigations policy responses solutions"),
408	)

411	def _stance_diversify_enabled() -> bool:
412	    """Env gate for the GENERAL stance/view-diversification seed lane, read WITHOUT importing anything so a
413	    flag-OFF run NEVER perturbs the query-gen path — byte-identical (the lane is simply skipped). Default
414	    OFF; accepts ``1``/``true``/``on``/``yes``. Read at CALL time (LAW VI). Mirrors
415	    ``_landmark_expander_enabled`` exactly."""
416	    return os.getenv("PG_STANCE_DIVERSIFY_SEEDS", "0").strip().lower() in ("1", "true", "on", "yes")

419	def _stance_diversify_seeds(facets: list[Any], question: str) -> list[str]:
420	    """Build the GENERAL stance-diversified query frontier for the planned facets.
421	
422	    For each facet, emit one query per generic STANCE frame (supporting / opposing / challenges /
423	    opportunities), each = ``{facet} {stance lens} {question anchor}`` — the facet supplies the
424	    sub-topic, the stance lens supplies the viewpoint, and the anchor (reused from the sibling
425	    expert-facet planner) keeps the query inside the question's subject so it cannot generalise into the
426	    facet's broad field (the same scope-anchor discipline R1/landmark use). Deterministic (no LLM), $0.
427	    Ordered, case-insensitively de-duplicated. Returns ``[]`` when there are no facets.
428	
429	    GENERAL, no benchmark-gaming: the stance lens words are topic-agnostic templates — no study title /
430	    country / benchmark topic is baked in. §-1.3: this ADDS on-topic queries only; it DROPS ZERO sources
431	    and CAPS NOTHING (no target count, no per-facet cap knob — the frontier size emerges from the fixed
432	    four frames x the facets); every emitted query still routes through the UNCHANGED per_query_retrieve
433	    + the frozen faithfulness engine — a stance query that finds nothing verifiable contributes nothing.
434	    """
435	    from src.polaris_graph.retrieval import expert_facet_planner as _efp
436	    anchor = _efp._question_anchor(question)
437	    out: list[str] = []
438	    seen: set[str] = set()
439	    for f in facets:
440	        name = (getattr(f, "name", "") or "").strip()
441	        if not name:
442	            continue
443	        for _label, lens in _STANCE_FRAMES:
444	            q = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", f"{name} {lens} {anchor}").strip()
445	            if not q:
446	                continue
447	            key = q.lower()
448	            if key in seen:
449	                continue
450	            seen.add(key)
451	            out.append(q)
452	    return out
453	
454	
455	def _lines(text: str, cap: int = 12) -> list[str]:
456	    """Parse an LLM reply into clean sub-topic / query line items (strip numbering/bullets)."""
457	    out: list[str] = []
458	    for raw in (text or "").splitlines():
459	        s = raw.strip()
460	        if not s:
461	            continue
462	        s = re.sub(r"^\s*(?:[-*]|\d+[.):])\s*", "", s).strip().strip('"').strip()
463	        if s and len(s) > 2 and not s.lower().startswith(("here", "sure", "the following")):
464	            out.append(s)
465	        if len(out) >= cap:
466	            break
467	    return out
468

470	def _is_status_leak(text: str, question: str) -> bool:
471	    """I-qgen-001 (#1373): True iff ``text`` is (or embeds) a validator/status message
472	    ("Temporal constraint violation: ...", "Insufficient pre-June ...", "Lack of
473	    specific ...") rather than a research sub-topic/query. Uses the SHARED
474	    ``is_meta_status_clause`` predicate the retrieval-side scope validator runs, so
475	    both legs share one classifier; the question text is the subject-exemption
476	    source (a marker the operator themselves wrote is genuine subject, not a leak).
477	    ``PG_QUERY_META_STATUS_SCREEN=0`` kill-switch => always False (byte-identical)."""
478	    from src.polaris_graph.retrieval.scope_query_validator import (  # noqa: PLC0415
479	        is_meta_status_clause,
480	        meta_status_screen_enabled,
481	    )
482	    return meta_status_screen_enabled() and is_meta_status_clause(text, question)

485	def _screen_status_lines(items: list[str], question: str) -> list[str]:
486	    """I-qgen-001 (#1373) channel separation: drop validator/status prose from an
487	    LLM-derived sub-topic (todo) list so a validation RESULT can never occupy the
488	    SUB-TOPIC {topic} slot of the query-derivation prompt (drb_72 ev_1091: the leak
489	    was interpolated into '... regarding the impact of Temporal constraint
490	    violation: The research question requires literature' and searched). A
491	    validation message is control-flow — the affected round's facet is ABORTED
492	    (dropped from the todo queue), never pasted into query text. Faithfulness-
493	    neutral: pre-fetch query channel only; drops ZERO fetched sources (§-1.3)."""
494	    kept = [s for s in items if not _is_status_leak(s, question)]
495	    n_dropped = len(items) - len(kept)
496	    if n_dropped:
497	        logger.info(
498	            "[fs_researcher] I-qgen-001 (#1373) status-leak screen dropped %d "
499	            "validator/status line(s) from the sub-topic queue "
500	            "(a validation message is never a query topic).",
501	            n_dropped,
502	        )
503	    return kept

506	def _retrieval_deadline_passed(deadline: "float | None") -> bool:
507	    """I-deepfix-001 WALL-03 (#1344): True iff the SHARED per-question retrieval wall
508	    (a monotonic instant anchored ONCE by the spine) has passed.
509	
510	    Mirrors ``run_honest_sweep_r3._question_retrieval_deadline_passed`` byte-for-byte:
511	    pure ``time.monotonic()`` comparison against the ALREADY-anchored deadline (never
512	    re-reads the env — that would re-anchor the wall per call). STRICT ``>`` so the
513	    boundary instant itself is not yet 'passed'. ``deadline is None`` (the DEFAULT — the
514	    spine passes None when ``PG_RETRIEVAL_QUESTION_WALL_SECONDS`` is unset) => always
515	    ``False`` => the FS-Researcher loop is unbounded exactly as before (byte-identical).
516	
517	    When set, the outer round-loop and the inner per-todo loop consult this so, once the
518	    wall passes, the loop STOPS issuing new GLM rounds (query-derivation + checklist
519	    critic — the rounds FIX-2's per-query-retrieve short-circuit does NOT cover) and
520	    returns the queries gathered so far. §-1.3: stops adding query rounds; drops NO
521	    gathered source; touches no faithfulness gate."""
522	    return deadline is not None and time.monotonic() > deadline
523

525	def _obs_digest(rows: list[dict], n: int = 3, chars: int = 160) -> str:
526	    """A short digest of the newest evidence rows (steers the checklist's gap analysis)."""
527	    parts = []
528	    for r in (rows or [])[:n]:
529	        txt = " ".join((r.get("statement") or r.get("direct_quote") or r.get("title") or "").split())[:chars]
530	        if txt:
531	            parts.append(txt)
532	    return " | ".join(parts)
